figures.caption: FIG. 3. Qutrit shift gate. (a) Illustration of a qutrit shift gate in an oscillator. Experimentally reconstructed Wigner functions are shown for six input states (top row) and the corresponding output states after the shift gate (bottom row). (b) Sequence used for joint qubit-oscillator tomography. The oscillator state is measured using Wigner tomography after postselecting on four qubit basis states. (c) Angles of the ancilla qubit and JC rotations, and JC detunings, for a qutrit shift gate. (d) Pulse sequence corresponding to the rotation angles in (c). (e) Populations P (|g, n⟩) and P (|f, n⟩) as a function of circuit layer, measured using joint qubit–oscillator tomography for a qutrit shift gate initialized in |g0⟩, |g1⟩, and |g2⟩. The markers show the experimentally measured populations and the lines display the simulated decoherence-free evolution.

FIG. 4. Process tomography of universal qutrit gates. Absolute value of the process matrix for a qutrit (a) shift gate X, (b) X2 gate, (c) Hadamard gate, (d) phase gate S, (e) T gate, and (f) π/2 Givens rotation in the |0⟩-|1⟩ subspace. The solid black lines indicate the ideal gate. The full process matrices are shown in Figs. S9 and S10 of the SI. (g) Process fidelities of the universal qutrit gates after post-selection are shown as solid blue markers. Grey markers indicate results of Lindblad master-equation simulations. The blue hollow markers show the process fidelity obtained when the implemented gate is compared to the target gate followed by a qudit phase gate, R(φ)Utarget, where R(φ) = ∑ j e ijφ |j⟩ ⟨j|. Error bars on the fidelities are obtained by extracting errors in the density matrix reconstruction from Wigner tomography following Ref. [15], and propagating them through the process matrix reconstruction. (h) Sequence used for qutrit process tomography.
